coredata.dc:title: A graphene quantum dots based dual-modal fluorometric and visualized detection of copper ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

Huang, Qihan

Luo, Zhishan
Wei, Yaojin
Chen, Zhijun

Zhang, Huihui
Shi, Hui
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we present a dual-modal fluorometric and visualized detection method for Cu2+ ion, leveraging the synergistic properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Cu2+ ion catalyzed Fenton-like reaction. The Fenton-like reaction of Cu2+ ions and ascorbate generates highly reactive hydroxyl radical (·OH), which effectively disrupt the structure of GQDs, leading to fluorescence quenching. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence quenching degree exhibited a linear correlation with Cu2+ concentration within the range of 40 to 2000 nM, enabling the detection of Cu2+ ions as low as 40 nM. Furthermore, we demonstrated the feasibility of semi-quantitative visual detection of Cu2+ ion concentrations in water using a portable ultraviolet instrument. The method achieved a minimum detectable concentration of Cu2+ ion as low as 10 μM, surpassing the maximum contaminant level goals of 20.47 μM set by the EPA and the guideline value of 31.47 μM recommended by the WHO. As such, this approach holds promise as a point-of-care testing (POCT) method for Cu2+ ion detection during copper pollution emergency events in water. Additionally, this method can be adapted for the detection of ascorbic acid. Our findings showcase the potential of this dual-modal detection approach, offering a sensitive, rapid, and efficient means for detecting Cu2+ ion, thereby contributing to environmental monitoring and public health applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Copper ion
Ascorbic acid
Hydroxyl radical

Detection of Cu2+ in a real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GQDs. (A) TEM image. (B) Size distributions of GQDs by DLS. (C) XRD patterns of GQDs. (D) AFM image. (E) FT-IR spectrum. (F) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectrum.

The optical properties of GQDs. (A) UV–vis absorption and excitation spectra of GQDs. The inset shows the photos of aqueous dispersion of GQDs under UV and fluorescent lamps irradiation. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQD solution under different excitation wavelength.
Feasibility of Cu2+ detection. Fluorescence changes of GQDs after mixing with different substances.

GQDs-based sensing of Cu2+. (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs/ascorbate with addition of different concentrations of Cu2+. (B) the corresponding relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Cu2+. (C) the photos of GQDs/ascorbate with addition of different concentrations of Cu2+ under UV (365 nm).

Specificity investigation of assay. (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs/ascorbate with addition of different metal ions. (B) Quenching ratio of different metal ions. (C) the photos of GQDs/ascorbate with addition of different metal ions.
Application of visualized detection in actual water samples.
Assay in Vc tablet. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs with different concentration of Vc tablet.
Schematic illustration of Fenton-like reaction and GQDs based strategy for sensing of Cu2+ ions.
Comparison of reported methods for the detection of Cu2+ ions based on nanomaterials.

head.ce:title: A graphene quantum dots based dual-modal fluorometric and visualized detection of copper ions

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, College of Biology, Hunan University, Key Laboratory for Bio-Nanotechnology and Molecular Engineering of Hunan Province, Changsha 410082, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a dual-modal fluorometric and visualized detection of copper ions was developed. Furthermore, this method can be adapted for the detection of ascorbic acid in medicine.

In this study, we present a dual-modal fluorometric and visualized detection method for Cu2+ ion, leveraging the synergistic properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Cu2+ ion catalyzed Fenton-like reaction. The Fenton-like reaction of Cu2+ ions and ascorbate generates highly reactive hydroxyl radical (·OH), which effectively disrupt the structure of GQDs, leading to fluorescence quenching. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence quenching degree exhibited a linear correlation with Cu2+ concentration within the range of 40 to 2000 nM, enabling the detection of Cu2+ ions as low as 40 nM. Furthermore, we demonstrated the feasibility of semi-quantitative visual detection of Cu2+ ion concentrations in water using a portable ultraviolet instrument. The method achieved a minimum detectable concentration of Cu2+ ion as low as 10 μM, surpassing the maximum contaminant level goals of 20.47 μM set by the EPA and the guideline value of 31.47 μM recommended by the WHO. As such, this approach holds promise as a point-of-care testing (POCT) method for Cu2+ ion detection during copper pollution emergency events in water. Additionally, this method can be adapted for the detection of ascorbic acid. Our findings showcase the potential of this dual-modal detection approach, offering a sensitive, rapid, and efficient means for detecting Cu2+ ion, thereby contributing to environmental monitoring and public health applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper, one of humanity's earliest utilized metals, finds widespread applications in electrical, light industry, machinery manufacturing, construction, national defense, and other modern industries. It also plays a vital role as a coenzyme factor (known as “cuproenzymes”) in various organisms, contributing to essential physiological processes in the human body, including iron metabolism, angiogenesis, gene expression regulation, and immune system functioning [1,2]. Unfortunately, in recent years, there have been frequent incidents of copper pollution. Excessive intake of Cu2+ ion, as a heavy metal, can lead to various diseases like Menkes and Wilson diseases, Alzheimer’s disease [3], as well as acute hepatic and kidney failure. In response to safeguarding public health, the World Health Organization (WHO) and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have imposed strict restrictions on Cu2+ ion levels in drinking water (or tap water), setting the limits at 2 mg/L (31.47 μM) and 1.3 mg/L (20.47 μM), respectively [4,5]. Therefore, regular monitoring of Cu2+ ion in drinking water is of utmost importance.

Numerous methods have been developed for detecting Cu2+ ion, including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [6], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [7], anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV) [8], and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9]. While these methods offer high reliability, precision, and accuracy, they have drawbacks such as being time-consuming, requiring expensive specialized equipment, and demanding highly qualified staff. Importantly, their test results exhibit a lag during water pollution emergency events, hindering the prompt implementation of emergency measures. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a simple, cost-effective, and highly sensitive method for detecting Cu2+ ion in water, ideally with point-of-care testing (POCT) capabilities, able to identify Cu2+ ion exceeding safety limits like the EPA-mandated 20 μM. In recent years, several new Cu2+ ion detection methods have emerged, encompassing fluorometry [10], colorimetry [11], electrochemistry [12], and more [13–15]. Nanomaterials have played a significant role in these innovative methods. For instance, semiconductor quantum dots [16,17], gold nanoclusters [18–20], silver nanoparticles [21], copper nanoparticles [22], and others [23–25] have been widely employed. However, many of these nanomaterials involve complex and costly preparation procedures or may pose toxicity concerns, making them unsuitable for routine testing. Therefore, there is a growing interest in exploring alternative methods that leverage the unique properties of nanomaterials while ensuring simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and safety for practical applications.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), zero-dimensional luminescent carbon nanomaterials, consist of one or a few layers of graphene sheets [26]. Their distinctive optical and electrical properties are attributed to quantum confinement and edge effects [27]. Remarkably, the optical properties of GQDs can be tailored by size or chemical element doping [28]. Moreover, GQDs offer significant advantages of low cost, high water solubility, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [29]. As a result, extensive research has been conducted to explore and apply GQDs in various fields, including sensing, catalysis, bioimaging, and medical diagnosis [30–32].

In this study, we developed a dual-modal fluorometric and visualized detection method for Cu2+ ions based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and a Fenton-like reaction. As depicted in Scheme 1 , the Fenton-like reaction between ascorbate (or ascorbic acid) and Cu2+ ions generated highly reactive hydroxyl radicals (·OH), known for their exceptional reactivity and potent oxidizing abilities [33]. The presence of ·OH caused the fragmentation of GQDs, resulting in a decrease in their fluorescence emission. This “turn-off” photoluminescent assay facilitated the detection of Cu2+ ion, and the quenching process was irreversible, unaffected by interfering substances, showcasing superior detection selectivity. Furthermore, with the aid of a portable ultraviolet instrument, visual semi-quantitative detection of Cu2+ ion could be achieved. It plays a crucial role in addressing copper pollution incidents. For example, when Cu2+ ions in drinking water exceed the maximum contaminant level goals (MCLG), our method enables point-of-care testing of Cu2+ ions, facilitate the timely initiation of emergency measures, and avoiding the spread of pollution caused by delayed detection. It holds substantial promise as a potential method for reliable Cu2+ ions assay, providing valuable insights into environmental monitoring and public health applications. In brief, this method enables the high-sensitivity detection of Cu2+ ions in water using fluorescence analysis, as well as rapid semi-quantitative detection through visual inspection.

Citric acid and 3-(N-morpholino) propanesulfonic acid (MOPS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Sodium ascorbate, sodium hydrate (NaOH), sodium chloride (NaCl), cupric sulfate (CuSO4·5H2O), silver nitrate (AgNO3), calcium chloride (CaCl2), ferrous chloride (FeCl2·4H2O), ferric chloride (FeCl3), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2·6H2O), manganese chloride (MnCl2·4H2O), cadmium chloride (CdCl2·2.5H2O), zinc acetate (ZnAc2·2H2O), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), and mercury nitrate (Hg(NO3)2) were obtained from Sinopharm chemical reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals used were of the highest grade available. All stock and buffer solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm from Millpore purification system).

GQDs were synthesized using a modified citric acid pyrolysis method. Initially, 2 g of citric acid (CA) was heated to molten at 200℃. After approximately 45 min of heating, when the melt acquired a brown color, it was carefully dropped into 100 mL of 10 mg·mL−1 NaOH aqueous solution while continuously stirring. Once the melt dissolved, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter and adjusted to neutrality. After aging at 4℃ for 3 days, the solution turned pale yellow. Subsequently, the GQDs solution was mixed with ethanol in a volume ratio of 1:3. The GQDs were collected by removing larger particles through centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and then dried under vacuum. The as-prepared GQDs were diluted in MOPS buffer (pH 7.50 @ 25℃: 10 mM MOPS, 0.15 M NaCl, 10 mM MgCl2) at a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL for further characterization and usage.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken using a JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patternswere measured by a Japan Rigaku SmartLab SE using Cu Kα radiation. Morphology of GQDs were determined on an atomic forcemicroscope (AFM) (Bruker Dimension Icon, Germany) using tapping mode to simultaneously collect height and phase data. The size distributions of GQDs measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90). A Nicolet iS20 spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was employed to collect the Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The surface composition of the GQDs were analyzed with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Beckman Coulter DU-800 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescent measurements were performed on an QM40-NIR fluorescence spectrophotometer (PTI, USA).

As Cu2+ detection, 2 mM ascorbic acid was added in GQDs. And the fluorescent measurements were performed on fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature. The excitation wavelength was set at 370 nm, with an emission wavelength range of 400–650 nm (max λem = 470 nm) and a slit width of 5 nm. For Cu2+ detection in practical water samples, water from a lake was first filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and then subjected to analysis using the standard addition method.

The morphology and size distribution of GQDs were studied by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscope (AFM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS). As depicted in TEM images of the GQDs (Fig. 1 A), the GQDs exhibited a spherical shape with an average diameter of approximately 2.15 nm, with a lattice spacing of ∼ 0.24 nm corresponding to plane of graphene [34]. And the size distributions of GQDs by DLS was about 3.4 ± 0.49 nm (Fig. 1B). The AFM result indicates that the height of GQDs were distributed in the range of 1 to 2.5 nm (2.1 nm average height, Fig. 1D), corresponding to an average layer number of 1 ∼ 3 [35]. The X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) patterns of GQDs show a wide peak around 0.29 nm (2θ = 30.01°, Fig. 1C), which was correspond to lattice fringe in TEM. Additionally, The chemical structure of GQDs was analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and several characteristic absorption peaks were observed in Fig. 1E, including a C–C bending vibration at 1250 cm−1, C–H bending vibration at 1406 cm−1, C = C stretching vibration at 1560 cm−1, and an intense and wide O–H peak at 3427 cm−1. A broad band at ∼ 1000 cm−1 corresponds to the C-O stretching. In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted to identify the surface composition and chemical environments of GQDs. The survey XPS spectrum shows two major peaks at 263, and 510 eV, which are assigned to C1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 1F). The peaks at 475 and 1050 eV were correspond to the elements of NaKLL, Na1s, which caused by impurity Na+. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Figure S1A) displays the strong signal of C = C at 284.2 eV and the distinguishable C-OH peak at 287.6 eV. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Figure S1B) reveals the presence of O–H at 530.6 eV and 534.9 eV. The results indicated that the GQDs were mainly composed of C and O, which were responded to FT-IR spectrum.

To characterize the optical properties, UV–vis spectrophotometry and fluorescence spectroscopy were utilized. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 2 A) displayed an absorption band around 360 nm, which closely aligns with the maximum excitation wavelength of GQDs (Fig. 2B). Under the irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp, the GQDs emitted vivid blue luminescence (Fig. 2A, inset). The fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs were illustrated in Fig. 2B. Unlike many excitation-dependent GQDs, the purified GQDs synthesized in this study exhibited excitation-independent behavior. The GQDs demonstrated a blue fluorescence emission peak at 480 nm with excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 nm to 430 nm, and the most intense fluorescence occurred at 370 nm excitation. Notably, besides their bright fluorescence, the GQDs exhibited remarkable stability. Even after continuous illumination for one hour, they maintained strong fluorescence (Figure S7, black line). Concurrently, the effect of pH on the fluorescence emission of GQDs was explored. The Figure S2 demonstrates that GQDs exhibit superior fluorescence in neutral and alkaline environments, whereas their fluorescence is significantly attenuated under acidic conditions, particularly under strongly acidic circumstances.

The hydroxyl radical (·OH), produced through the Fenton-like reaction of Cu2+ and ascorbate, plays a key role in oxidatively damaging the GQDs, resulting in the attenuation of their fluorescence. To demonstrate the feasibility of our sensing strategy, we conducted studies under different conditions. As depicted in Fig. 3 , the as-prepared GQDs exhibited remarkable photostability when Cu2+ ions or ascorbic acid were introduced alone (indicated by the first arrows on the red and green curves). However, when both Cu2+ ions and ascorbate coexisted in the system (shown by the yellow curve or the second arrow on the red and green curves), the fluorescence of GQDs started to decrease. Within the initial 10 min, the high concentrations of Cu2+ ions and ascorbate, along with the substantial production of hydroxyl radicals, caused a rapid decline in GQDs fluorescence, reducing the intensity by over 40 %. As the reaction proceeded, the concentration of hydroxyl radicals decreased, leading to a slower decline rate of GQDs fluorescence. Within the subsequent 30 min, the fluorescence quenching rate reached 90 %. Additionally, the concentrations of ascorbate and Cu2+ directly influenced the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. With increasing ascorbate concentration, the attenuation rate of GQDs fluorescence gradually accelerated. As shown in Figure S3, when the ascorbate concentration exceeded 2 mM, there was no significant difference in the fluorescence decay rate of GQDs. Therefore, 2 mM ascorbate was applied as the optimal condition in the subsequent Cu2+ ions detection. Additionally, in order to further validate the detection mechanism, sulfur ions (S2−) and N-Acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC) were employed as inhibitors of Cu2+ ions and ·OH, respectively. As depicted in Figure S4, upon introduction of 200 μM S2− into the detection system, Cu2+ ions underwent conversion into copper sulfide precipitation (CuS), which rendered them unreactive with ascorbate and consequently prevented quenching of GQDs' fluorescence. N-Acetyl-L-cysteine is commonly utilized for inhibiting reactive oxygen species (ROS). Upon addition of 200 μM NAC, the ·OH generated by the reaction between Cu2+ ion and ascorbate could be effectively eliminated, thereby maintaining a strong fluorescence in GQDs. Furthermore, we also investigated the impact of other ROS, such as hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) on GQD's fluorescence. As illustrated in the Figure S5, after 30 min H2O2 was able to partially quench the fluorescence of GQDs; however, its quenching efficiency was significantly lower compared to that exerted by ·OH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of Cu2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the detection sensitivity, we monitored the fluorescence changes after the addition of Cu2+ for a fixed duration of 15 min The fluorescent responses of the GQDs following the titration of Cu2+ were depicted in Fig. 4 A. As the Cu2+ concentration increased, the fluorescence signals gradually decreased until reaching a plateau at 10 μM. Notably, there was a strong linear correlation between fluorescence quenching rate (ΔF) and Cu2+ concentrations in the range of 40–2000 nM (Fig. 4B, R2 = 0.9912), with the lowest concentration detected being 40 nM. The limit of detection (LOD) value is significantly lower than the maximum allowable level of Cu2+ (20 μM) in tap water, as regulated by the EPA. Furthermore, by visually observing the physical photos under the ultraviolet lamp, we could clearly discern the process of GQDs fluorescence intensity changing with the concentration of Cu2+. The Cu2+ ion concentration in water can be detected semi-quantitatively by colorimetric method with standard series under UV. Upon adding 10 μM Cu2+ to the GQDs solution, the blue fluorescence of the GQDs was nearly completely quenched. Hence, this method can also serve as a visual point-of-care testing (POCT) to determine whether Cu2+ in drinking water are at risk of exceeding the maximum contaminant level goals of 20 μM.

The selectivity toward Cu2+ ions was further investigated in the presence of other metal ions (Na+, K+, Cd2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+), as shown in Fig. 5 . Upon adding metal ions (10 μM), no significant fluorescence intensity changes were observed. However, upon further addition of ascorbate into the above mixtures, a complete fluorescence quenching was observed specifically in the presence of Cu2+. Notably, Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions also exhibited the ability to quench the fluorescence of GQDs due to their Fenton-like reaction with ascorbate. Nevertheless, the efficiency of ascorbate oxidation by Cu2+ was found to be higher than by Fe2+ and Fe3+ [36]. Consequently, the decrease in GQDs fluorescence intensity caused by Cu2+ was more pronounced compared to Fe2+ and Fe3+. By implementing a masking method, selective detection of Cu2+ ions can be achieved. Oxidizing Fe2+ into Fe3+ and subsequently eliminating the influence of Fe3+ using masking agents such as triethanolamine (TEA) or sodium hexametaphosphate allowed us to selectively detect Cu2+ ions. Figure S6 illustrates that the addition of 6 mM triethanolamine (TEA) to the previously mentioned Fe3+ solution, followed by its reaction with ascorbate, does not result in the quenching of the GQDs' fluorescence. This observation suggests that the incorporation of TEA significantly enhances the selectivity for Cu2+ ion detection.

Furthermore, a comparison of our method with previously reported nanomaterial-based methods for the detection of Cu2+ ions was presented in Table 1 . While the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) method demonstrates excellent detection sensitivity [18], its complex modification process limits its suitability for POCT. In contrast, our method offers significant advantages, including excellent detection sensitivity, convenience, low cost, environmental friendliness, and the capability of visual semi-quantitative detection, making it a promising option for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in a real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical applicability of our developed assay, we tested water samples collected from Taozi Lake in Hunan province, China, using the standard addition method. The water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and then spiked with various concentrations of Cu2+(400 nM, 800 nM, 1500 nM). As indicated in Table 2 , the sample recoveries ranged from 84.4 % to 108 %, demonstrating the robustness and potential of our method for practical applications. And the visual detection of the corresponding concentration of Cu2+ ions is illustrated in the Fig. 6 . In the standard series, an increase in Cu2+ ions concentration is accompanied by a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity. In actual water sample detection, the fluorescence of 400 nM Cu2+ ions was consistent with that of 400 nM in the standard series. Similarly, the fluorescence of 800 nM and 1500 nM Cu2+ ions correspond to the ranges of 1000–400 nM and 2000–1000 nM, respectively. This result demonstrates the practical utility of visual detection.

Furthermore, our developed strategy also enables the determination of vitamin C (Vc). By maintaining a fixed concentration of Cu2+ at 200 μM (Figure. S7), we observed a gradual decrease in the fluorescence of GQDs with increasing concentrations of Vc. Figure. S8 illustrates that the fluorescence quenching rate (ΔF) exhibited a linear correlation with Vc concentration in the range of 2 to 200 μM (R2 = 0.9956). Moreover, we applied this method to detect Vc tablets. The tablets were ground into powder and dissolved in water. After centrifugation (10000 rpm), different concentrations of Vc were prepared as sample solutions. As depicted in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence intensity of GQDs weakened with increasing Vc addition. Calculations indicated that the content of Vc in the 0.012 mg·mL−1 and 0.024 mg·mL−1 Vc tablets sample solutions was 1.48 μg and 2.74 μg, respectively, consistent with the content specified in the tablet instructions (1.2 μg and 2.4 μg). These results highlight the applicability of our method for practical Vc detection.

In conclusion, we have successfully developed a novel dual-modal fluorometric and visualized detection method for Cu2+ ions based on quenching behavior of GQDs by hydroxyl radicals (·OH). The GQDs exhibit excellent photoluminescent properties, which are significantly attenuated upon exposure to ·OH generated through a Fenton-like reaction involving Cu2+ ion and ascorbate. Benefits from the recyclability of Cu2+ ions, the detection system demonstrates exceptional sensitivity and selective detection in a wide concentration range, with a limit of detection as low as 40 nM. The use of GQDs as the sensing platform offers several advantages, including their excellent photostability, low cost, and biocompatibility. Moreover, the UV-excited luminescence property of GQDs allows for visual and semi-quantitative detection under a portable ultraviolet instrument, enabling real-time and on-site analysis. This feature proves to be crucial for monitoring Cu2+ ion concentrations in drinking water and environmental samples, especially when they exceed the maximum contaminant level goals (MCLG) set by regulatory agencies like EPA. Furthermore, the versatility of this approach extends beyond Cu2+ ion detection, as it can also be utilized for the determination of ascorbic acid. The simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and high sensitivity of this method make it a promising tool for a wide range of applications, including environmental monitoring, water quality assessment, and biomedical diagnostics. In summary, our method stands out as a simple, cost-effective, and highly sensitive approach for Cu2+ ion detection. The combination of fluorometric and visualized detection, along with the use of readily available equipment, enhances its potential for practical applications in environmental monitoring and water quality assessment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Science and Technology Planning Project of Putian University (2019116); the Project of Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2022 N5006, 2023 J011003, 2023 J05055, 2024 J01878); Science and Technology Plan Project of Putian (2023GJGZ001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125442.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Biomed. Pharmacother.

Chem. Rev.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cloud point extraction and flame atomic absorption spectrometry combination for copper(II) ion in environmental and biological samples
J. Hazard. Mater.
